questions.question: If A and B are two sets, then $A \cap (A \cup B)'$ is equal to

$A \cap (A \cup B)' = \phi $ 
Since $(A \cup B)'$ Set represent the element which are not belongs to it, so there is no common element with the set A. So the answer is null set.

questions.question: Let A and B be two sets such that $n(A)=35,n(B)=42\quad and\quad n(A\cap B)=17$, find $n(A-B)$
questions.question_raw: Let A and B be two sets such that {tex}n(A)=35,n(B)=42\quad and\quad n(A\cap B)=17{/tex}, find {tex}n(A-B){/tex}
questions.explanation: $​​​​Given\quad that\quad n(A\cap B)=17\\ We\quad have\quad A-B=A-(A\cap B)\\ Therefore\quad n(A-B)=n(A)-n(A\cap B)=35-17=18$

questions.question: If A and B be any two sets, then (A $\cup$ B)' is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A and B be any two sets, then (A {tex}\cup{/tex} B)' is equal to
questions.explanation:
x $\in$ (A $\cup$ B)'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ (A $\cup$ B)
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ A or x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A' and x $\in$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A' $\cap$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ (A $\cup$ B)' $\subseteq$ A' $\cap$ B'
Similarly, we can show (A' $\cap$ B') $\subseteq$ (A $\cup$ B)'
So (A $\cup$ B)' = (A' $\cap$ B')

questions.question: If A and B be any two sets, then (A $\cap$ B)' is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A and B be any two sets, then (A {tex}\cap{/tex} B)' is equal to
questions.explanation:
x $\in$ A' $\cup$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A' or x $\in$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A or x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A $\cap$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ (A $\cap$ B)
Hence (A $\cap$ B)' = A' $\cup$ B'

questions.question_raw:
Which of the following is a null set?

  1. {x : x2 + 1 = 0, x {tex}\in{/tex} R}
  2. {x : x > 0 or x < 0}
  3. {x : x2 = 4 or x = 3}
  4. {0}
questions.explanation:
x^2 + 1 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 = -1
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 = $\sqrt{-1}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = i $\in$ C
[i = imaginary root of unity which is a complex no.]
Since the solution of x does not belong to real no hence the set is null set.

otin (B \cup C) \cr & \Rightarrow x \in A\;and(x

questions.question: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A – (B $\mathop \cap \nolimits $ C) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A – (B {tex}\mathop \cap \nolimits {/tex} C) is equal to
questions.explanation:
A - (B $\cap$ C) = (A - B) $\cup$ (A - C)
Proof:
Use following properties:
X - Y = X $\cap$ Y'
(X $\cap$ Y)' = X' $\cup$ Y' 
A - (B $\cap$ C) = A $\cap$ (B $\cap$ C)'
= A $\cap$ (B' $\cup$ C') = (A $\cap$ B') $\cup$ (A $\cap$ C') = (A - B) $\cup$ (A - C)

questions.question: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A $\mathop \cap \nolimits $ (B $\mathop \cup \nolimits $ C) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A, B and C are any three sets, then A {tex}\mathop \cap \nolimits {/tex} (B {tex}\mathop \cup \nolimits {/tex} C) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let x $\in$ A $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C)
x $\in$ A or x $\in$ (B and C)
x $\in$ A or {x $\in$ B and x $\in$ C}
{x $\in$ (A or B) and x $\in$ (A or C)
x $\in$ (A or B) $\cap$ x $\in$ (A or C)
x $\in$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ x $\in$ (A $\cap$ C)
x $\in$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C)
Therefore, A $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C) $\subset$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C) .....(i)
Similarly,
(A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C) $\subset$ A $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C) .....(ii)
So
A $\cup$ (B $\cup$ C) = (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ (A $\cup$ C)

questions.question: P(A) = P(B) $ \Rightarrow $
questions.question_raw: P(A) = P(B) {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}
questions.explanation:
To prove A = B it is enough to prove B $\subseteq$ A and A $\subseteq$ B
So let P(A) = P(B)
Also let x $\in$ A
Now we have A $\in$ P(A)
$\Rightarrow$ A $\in$ P(B), since P(A) = P(B)
$\therefore$ x $\in$ E for some E $\in$ P(B)
Now E $\subset$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ B
Hence we proved A $\subseteq$ B
Similarly by taking x $\in$ B and showing x $\in$ A we get B $\subseteq$ A
Hence A = B

questions.question: If A $\subset$ B, then
questions.question_raw: If A {tex}\subset{/tex} B, then
questions.explanation:
Let A $\subset$ B
To prove B^c $\subset$ A^c, it is enough to show that x $\in$ B^c $\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A^c 
Let x $\in$ B^c
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ A since A $\subset$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A^c
Hence B^c $\subset$ A^c

questions.question: A - B is equal to
questions.question_raw: A - B is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let x $\in$ A - B $\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ A^c and x $\in$ B^c 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ B^c and x $\notin$ A^c
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ B^c - A^c
$\Rightarrow$ A - B $\subset$ B^c - A^c .....(i)
Now let some y $\in$ B^c - A^c
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ B^c and x $\in$ A^c
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ B and x $\in$ A
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A - B
$\Rightarrow$ B^c - A^c $\subset$ A - B ......(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get A - B = B^c - A^c

questions.question: If A, B, C be any three sets such that $A\cup B=A\cup C$ and $A\mathop \cap \nolimits B = A\mathop \cap \nolimits C$, then
questions.question_raw: If A, B, C be any three sets such that {tex}A\cup B=A\cup C{/tex} and {tex}A\mathop \cap \nolimits B = A\mathop \cap \nolimits C{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
A $\cup$ B = A $\cup$ C
$\Rightarrow$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ C = (A $\cup$ C) $\cap$ C
$\Rightarrow$ (A $\cap$ C) $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C) = C .....(i)
Now again A $\cup$ B = A $\cup$ C
$\Rightarrow$ (A $\cup$ B) $\cap$ B = (A $\cup$ C) $\cap$ B
$\Rightarrow$ B = (A $\cap$ B) $\cup$ (C $\cap$ B) 
$\Rightarrow$ B = (A $\cap$ C) $\cup$ (C $\cap$ B), Since (A $\cap$ B) = (A $\cap$ C)
$\Rightarrow$ (A $\cap$ C) $\cup$ (B $\cap$ C) = B .....(ii)
Now from (i) and (ii) we get B = C

questions.explanation:
n(A) = 3
n(B) = 6
n(AUB) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A$\begin{array}{l}\cap\\\end{array}$B)
for minimum number of elements in ${A \cup B}$ 
n(A$\begin{array}{l}\cap\\\end{array}$B) = 3
So n(A $\cup$ B) = 6

questions.question: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The total number of subsets of the first set is 56 more than the total number of subsets of the second set. The values of m and n are
questions.question_raw: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The total number of subsets of the first set is 56 more than the total number of subsets of the second set. The values of m and n are
questions.explanation:
Let A and B be two sets having m and n elements respectively. Then,
Number of subsets of A = 2^m, Number of subsets of B = 2^n
It is given that 2^m - 2^n = 56
So, 2^n(2^m-n - 1) = 2^3(2^3 - 1)
n = 3 and m - n = 3 $ \Rightarrow $ n = 3 and m = 6.

Hence (A $\cup$ B) - (A $\cap$ B) = (A - B) $\cup$ (B - A)

questions.question: In a class of 100 students, 55 students have passed in Mathematics and 67 students have passed in Physics. Then the number of students who have passed in Physics only is
questions.question_raw: In a class of 100 students, 55 students have passed in Mathematics and 67 students have passed in Physics. Then the number of students who have passed in Physics only is
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of students of the class and let M and P denote the sets of students who passed in mathematics and physics respectively. Then
n(U) = 100, n(M) = 55 and n(P) = 67
Since all the students have passed in any of these subjects, we have 
n(U) = 100 $\Rightarrow$ n(M $\cup$ P) = 100
Now we have, n(M $\cup$ P) = n(M) + n(P) - n(M $\cap$ P)
$\Rightarrow$ 100 = 55 + 67 - n(M $\cap$ P)
$\Rightarrow$ n(M $\cap$ P) = 122 - 100 = 22
Which means the number of students who passed in both the subjects = 22
Hence the number of students who passed only in physics = n(P) - n(M $\cap$ P) = 67 - 22 = 45

questions.question: Out of 800 boys in a school, 224 played cricket, 240 played hockey and 336 played basketball. Of the total, 64 played both basketball and hockey, 80 played cricket and basketball and 40 played cricket and hockey, 24 played all the three games. The number of boys who did not play any game is:
questions.question_raw: Out of 800 boys in a school, 224 played cricket, 240 played hockey and 336 played basketball. Of the total, 64 played both basketball and hockey, 80 played cricket and basketball and 40 played cricket and hockey, 24 played all the three games. The number of boys who did not play any game is:
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of boys in a school and let C, H and B denote the sets of boys who played Cricked, Hockey and Basketball respectively.
Then we have n(U) = 800, n(C) = 224, n(H) = 240 and n(B) = 336
Also $n(C \cap H)=40 \quad, n(B \cap H)=64,$ $n(C \cap B)=80$ and $n(C \cap B \cap H)=24$
Now we have $n(C \cup H \cup B)=n(C)+n(H)+n(B)$$-n(C \cap H)-n(B \cap H)-n(C \cap B)+n(C \cap B \cap H)$
$\Rightarrow n(C \cup H \cup B)=$ 224 + 240 + 336 - 40 - 64 - 80 + 24
$\Rightarrow n(C \cup H \cup B)=640$
Which means the number of boys who play any one game = 640
Hence the number of boys who Did not play any game $=n(U)-n(C \cup H \cup B)=800-640=160$

questions.question: A survey shows that 75% of the Indians like apples, whereas 68% like oranges. What percentage of Indians like both apples and oranges.
questions.question_raw: A survey shows that 75% of the Indians like apples, whereas 68% like oranges. What percentage of Indians like both apples and oranges.
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of Indians and let A and B denote the sets of Indians who like apples and oranges respectively. Then
n(U) = 100, n(A) = 75 and n(B) = 68
We have n(U) = 100 $\Rightarrow$ n(A $\cup$ B) = 100
So n(A $\cup$ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A $\cap$ B)
$\Rightarrow$ 100 = 75 + 68 - n(M $\cap$ P)
$\Rightarrow$ n(M $\cap$ P) = 143 - 100 = 43
Which means 43 percentage of Indians like apples and oranges.

questions.question: The number of non-empty subsets of the set {1, 2, 3, 4} is:
questions.question_raw: The number of non-empty subsets of the set {1, 2, 3, 4} is:
questions.explanation:
Total no. of subset including empty set = 2^n 
So total subset = 2^4 = 16
The no. of non empty set = 16 - 1 = 15

questions.question: Which of the following is (A – B) $\mathop \cup \nolimits $ (A – C)?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is (A – B) {tex}\mathop \cup \nolimits {/tex} (A – C)?
questions.explanation:
Let $x\in A-(B\cap C)\Rightarrow x\in A\quad $and x $\notin$ B $\cap$ C
$\Rightarrow x{ \in A }$ and ($x\notin B$ or $x\notin { A }$)
$\Rightarrow$ (x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B) or (x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ C)
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A − B or x $\in$ A − C
$\Rightarrow$ x$\in$ (A − B) $\cup$ (A−C)
$\Rightarrow$ A − (B $\cap$ C) $\subset$ (A − B) $\cup$ (A-C) ......(i)
Now let y ∈ (A−B) ∪ (A−C)
$\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ A−B or y $\in$ A − C
$\Rightarrow$ (y $\in$ A and y ∉ B) or (y $\in$ A and y$\notin$ C)
$\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ A and (y$\notin$ B or y$\notin$ A)
$\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ A and y $\notin$ B $\cap$ C
$\Rightarrow$ y ∈ A − (B $\cap$ C) 
$\Rightarrow$ (A − B) $\cup$ (A − C) $\subset$ A − (B $\cap$ C)......(ii)
From (i) and (ii) we get A − (B $\cap$ C) = (A−B) $\cup$ (A − C)

A $\cap$ B^c

A $\cap$ B is the common region in both the sets.
(A $\cap$ B^c) is all the region in the universal set except A $\cap$ B
Now, A $\cap$ (A $\cap$ B)^c = A $\cap$ B^c

n(A $\mathop \cup 
olimits $ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A $\cap$ B)

questions.question: A survey shows that 63% of Americans like cheese whereas 76% like apples. If x% of the Americans like both cheese and apples, then the value of x is
questions.question_raw: A survey shows that 63% of Americans like cheese whereas 76% like apples. If x% of the Americans like both cheese and apples, then the value of x is
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of Americans and Lete A and B denote the sets of Americans who like cheese and apples respectively. Then
$n(U)=100$, n(A) = 62 and n(B) = 76
We have n(U) = 100 $\Rightarrow n(A \cup B)=100$
So $n(A \cup B)=n(A)+n(B)-n(A \cap B)$
$\Rightarrow 100=63+76-n(A \cap B)$
$\Rightarrow n(A \cap B)=139-100=39$
Which means 39 percent of Americans like Cheese and apples.

questions.question: In a town of 10,000 families it was found that 40% families buys newspaper A, 20% buy newspaper B, and 10% families buy newspaper C, 5% families buy A and B, 3% buy B and C and 4% buy A and C. If 2% buy all the three newspapers, then number of families which buy A only is
questions.question_raw: In a town of 10,000 families it was found that 40% families buys newspaper A, 20% buy newspaper B, and 10% families buy newspaper C, 5% families buy A and B, 3% buy B and C and 4% buy A and C. If 2% buy all the three newspapers, then number of families which buy A only is
questions.explanation:
Let U denote the set of families who buy news paper and let X, Y and Z denote the sets of families who buy newspaper A, B and C respectively
Then we have n(U) = 10000, n(X) = 40% of 10000 = 4000, n(Y) = 20% of 10000 = 2000 and n(Z) = 10% of 10000 = 1000
Also n(X $\cap$ Y) = 5% of 10000 = 500, n(Y $\cap$ Z) = 3% of 10000 = 300
n(X $\cap$ Z) = 4% of 10000 = 400 and (X $\cap$ Y $\cap$ Z) = 2% of 10000 = 200
Hence the number of families who buy only newspaper A = n[X - (Y $\cup$ Z)]
= n(X) - n[X $\cap$(Y $\cup$ Z)]
= n(X) - n[X $\cap$(Y $\cup$ Z)]
= n(X) - [n(X $\cap$ Y) + n(X $\cap$ Z) - n((X $\cap$ Y) $\cap$(X $\cap$ Z))]
= n(X) - [n(X $\cap$ Y) + n(X $\cap$ Z) - n(X $\cap$ Y $\cap$ Z)]
= 4000 - [500 + 400 - 200]
= 4000 - 700 = 3300

questions.question: In a battle 70% of combatants lost one eye, 80% an ear, 75% an arm, 85% a leg, x% lose all the four limbs. The minimum value of x is:
questions.question_raw: In a battle 70% of combatants lost one eye, 80% an ear, 75% an arm, 85%  a leg, x% lose all the four limbs. The minimum value of x is:
questions.explanation:
Given that percentage of combatants who lost one eye = 70%, an ear = 80% and an arm = 75% and a leg = 85%
Hence we get the percentage of combatants who lost one eye and one ear = (70% + 80%) - 100% = 50%
Percentage of combatants who lost one eye, one ear and one arm = (50% + 75%) - 100% = 25%
Similarly, the percentage of combatants who lost one eye, one ear, one arm, and one leg = (25% + 85%) - 100% = 10%
Hence we get the percentage of the combatants who lost all 4 limbs = 10%
Hence if x% represents the percentage of combatants who lost all 4 limbs we get the minimum value of x = 10

questions.question: Let A and B be two non- empty subsets of a set X such that A is not a subset of B, then
questions.question_raw: Let A and B be two non- empty subsets of a set X such that A is not a subset of B, then

options.label: A
options.text: A and B are always disjoint

options.label: B
options.text: A is always a subset of B

options.label: C
options.text: A and the complement of B are always non-disjoint

options.label: D
options.text: B is always a subset of A

correct_option_texts: A and the complement of B are always non-disjoint
questions.explanation:
Let x $\in$ A, then x $\notin$ B as A is not a subset of B
$\therefore$ x $\in$ A and x $\notin$ B
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A and x $\in$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ x $\in$ A $\cap$ B'
$\Rightarrow$ A and B' are non - disjoint.

Venn Diagram

questions.question: If A = {(x, y) : x^2 + y^2 = 25} and B = {(x, y) : x^2 + 9y^2 = 144} then A $\mathop \cap \nolimits $ B contains
questions.question_raw: If A = {(x, y) : x2 + y2 = 25} and B = {(x, y) : x2 + 9y2 = 144} then A {tex}\mathop \cap \nolimits {/tex} B contains
questions.explanation:
From A, x^2 + y^2 = 25 and from B, x^2 + 9y^2 = 144
$\therefore$ From B, (x^2 + y^2) + 8y^2 = 144
$ \Rightarrow$ 25 + 8y^2 = 144
$ \Rightarrow$ 8y^2 = 119 
$\Rightarrow$y = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{119}{8}}$ 
$\therefore$ x^2 + y^2 = 25 $\Rightarrow$ x^2 = 25 - y^2 = 25 - $\frac{119}{8}$ = $\frac{81}{8}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{81}{8}}$ 
Since we solved equations simultaneously, therefore ${{\text{A}} \cap \;{\text{B}}}$ has four points A has 2 elements & B has 2 elements.

questions.question: In a city 20% of the population travels by car, 50% travels by bus and 10% travels by both car and bus. Then persons travelling by car or bus is
questions.question_raw: In a city 20% of the population travels by car, 50%  travels by bus and 10% travels by both car and bus. Then persons travelling by car or bus is
questions.explanation:
Suppose C and B represents the population travel by car and Bus respectively.
n(C $\cup$ B) = n(C) + n(B) - n(B $\cap$ C)
= 0.20 + 0.50 - 0.10
= 0.6 or 60
